questions.question: What is FName?
options: Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
questions.correctAnswer: Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
questions.explanation: FName: hash table index. Comparing two FNames = comparing integers. Used for asset paths, identifiers. Case-insensitive.

o: Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.
questions.a: Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.
questions.e: FText = user-facing, localizable. FString = mutable, gameplay use. FName = interned, identifiers. Three distinct use cases.

o: UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.
questions.a: UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.
questions.e: TCHAR abstracts platform encoding. TEXT("hello") = L"hello" on Windows, "hello" elsewhere. FString is TArray<TCHAR>.

o: FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)
questions.a: FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)
questions.e: FName = (index, number). Index into global TNameEntryArray. == operator compares indices. String comparison is never done at runtime.

questions.q: What is the FName pool and why does it matter?
o: A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).
questions.a: A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).
questions.e: FNames live forever in the global pool. Don't create FNames from dynamic strings (player names, chat messages). Use FString for runtime-generated text.

questions.question: Why does UE use MoveTemp instead of std::move?
options: UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
questions.correctAnswer: UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
questions.explanation: UE naming convention: MoveTemp for rvalue cast. MoveTempIfPossible for conditional move. All wrap standard move semantics.

o: UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.
questions.a: UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.
questions.e: MoveTemp = UE naming for std::move. MoveTempIfPossible = conditional move. Same underlying mechanics as standard move semantics.

o: Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.
questions.a: Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.
questions.e: MoveTempIfPossible checks TIsMoveConstructible<T>. Falls back to copy for non-movable types. Used in TArray growth path.

questions.q: Why is TArray always able to move on reallocation?
o: UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.
questions.a: UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.
questions.e: No exceptions = no exception-safety concern. TArray doesn't need move_if_noexcept. Always moves elements on reallocation.

questions.q: What is FORCEINLINE and why is it used on MoveTemp?
o: Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.
questions.a: Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.
questions.e: MoveTemp is a template that MUST be inlined. FORCEINLINE = __forceinline on MSVC, always_inline on Clang. Zero function call overhead.

questions.question: What is TObjectPtr?
options: GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
questions.correctAnswer: GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
questions.explanation: TObjectPtr<T> replaces raw T* in UE5. Implicit conversion to T*. GC tracking. Lazy object resolution. Editor cook integration.

o: Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr
questions.a: Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr
questions.e: TSharedPtr: non-UObject ref-counting. TSharedRef: non-nullable variant. TWeakPtr: non-owning observer. For UObjects use TObjectPtr.

o: Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.
questions.a: Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.
questions.e: TUniquePtr<T> = exclusive heap ownership. Cannot be copied. Move transfers ownership. Automatically deletes on destruction.

o: Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.
questions.a: Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.
questions.e: TWeakObjectPtr = observer. Doesn't keep UObject alive. Used for optional references, back-pointers that shouldn't prevent GC.

questions.q: Why can't you use std::shared_ptr for UObjects?
o: UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.
questions.a: UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.
questions.e: UObject lifetime is managed by UE's garbage collector. Adding shared_ptr refcounting would create two competing ownership systems.

questions.question: What is TArray's growth strategy?
options: Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
questions.correctAnswer: Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
questions.explanation: TArray = dynamic array. Similar to vector. Grow by adding slack (default 4 elements at small sizes). Reserve() prevents reallocation.

questions.q: What does TArray::Emplace do vs Add?
o: Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.
questions.a: Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.
questions.e: Emplace like emplace_back. Avoids temporary creation. TArray::Emplace(Args...) forwards to element constructor at new end position.

o: Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds
questions.a: Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds
questions.e: Reserve(N) = allocate N slots upfront. Push N elements without reallocation. Same as std::vector::reserve.

questions.q: What is TArray's growth strategy when full?
o: Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.
questions.a: Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.
questions.e: UE adds Slack (default=4) each growth. Also grows by old_capacity. Larger arrays grow faster. Amortized O(1) Add like std::vector.

o: Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess
questions.a: Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess
questions.e: SetNum(N) = resize to N. If N > size: default-construct new elements. If N < size: destroy excess elements. Does NOT change capacity.

questions.question: What data structure does TMap use?
options: Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
questions.correctAnswer: Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
questions.explanation: TMap = unordered hash map. Uses sparse array with chaining. Key must have GetTypeHash(). No ordering guarantees. O(1) average lookup.

o: Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.
questions.a: Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.
questions.e: FVector* val = Map.Find(key); if(val) use(*val); Standard UE pattern — pointer-based lookup, no exceptions.

questions.q: What hash function does TMap require?
o: GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.
questions.a: GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.
questions.e: TMap requires GetTypeHash(const T&). Built-in types, FString, FName have it. Custom structs: add friend uint32 GetTypeHash(const FMyStruct&).

o: Returns reference to value for key, creating default-constructed value if key doesn't exist
questions.a: Returns reference to value for key, creating default-constructed value if key doesn't exist
questions.e: FindOrAdd: returns existing value or creates new entry. Useful for counters, caches. TMap[Key] = TMap::FindOrAdd(Key) + 1 style patterns.

questions.q: How is TMap different from TMultiMap?
o: TMap: one value per key (unique). TMultiMap: multiple values per key (duplicate keys allowed).
questions.a: TMap: one value per key (unique). TMultiMap: multiple values per key (duplicate keys allowed).
questions.e: TMap: unique keys. TMultiMap: multiple entries with same key. TMultiMap::Find returns first match. Use for key→many relationships.

questions.question: What does UHT (Unreal Header Tool) do?
options: Parses UCLASS/USTRUCT/UFUNCTION macros and generates reflection data — .generated.h files with StaticClass(), serialization, GC info
questions.correctAnswer: Parses UCLASS/USTRUCT/UFUNCTION macros and generates reflection data — .generated.h files with StaticClass(), serialization, GC info
questions.explanation: UHT pre-processes headers before the compiler. UCLASS→StaticClass(), class flags. UPROPERTY→serialization, replication. UFUNCTION→network RPC, Blueprint.

o: Marks member for GC tracking, serialization, replication, Blueprint access — UHT generates reflection code for marked properties
questions.a: Marks member for GC tracking, serialization, replication, Blueprint access — UHT generates reflection code for marked properties
questions.e: UPROPERTY = reflection + GC. Without it: no GC tracking (dangling!), no serialization (can't save/load), no Blueprint/network access.

o: Marks function for Blueprint call, network RPC, or exec command — UHT generates thunk code for cross-boundary calls
questions.a: Marks function for Blueprint call, network RPC, or exec command — UHT generates thunk code for cross-boundary calls
questions.e: UFUNCTION enables BlueprintCallable, Server/Client/NetMulticast RPCs, Exec (console). UHT generates thunks that marshal parameters.

questions.q: What is the difference between BlueprintCallable and BlueprintPure?
o: Callable: has execution pin (can modify state). Pure: no exec pin (can't modify), evaluated on demand like a getter.
questions.a: Callable: has execution pin (can modify state). Pure: no exec pin (can't modify), evaluated on demand like a getter.
questions.e: BlueprintCallable = void/impure function (white exec pins). BlueprintPure = const function (green, no pins). Use Pure for getters, math.

questions.q: What are the UFUNCTION specifiers for networking?
questions.correctAnswer: Server, Client, NetMulticast, Reliable, Unreliable, WithValidation
questions.explanation: Server: client→server. Client: server→owning client. NetMulticast: server→all. Reliable: guaranteed delivery. WithValidation: adds _Validate check.

questions.question: What is UObject?
options: Base class for all UE objects — provides GC, serialization, reflection, networking. Everything inherits from UObject.
questions.correctAnswer: Base class for all UE objects — provides GC, serialization, reflection, networking. Everything inherits from UObject.
questions.explanation: UObject = UE's base. AActor, UActorComponent, UAsset all derive from UObject. GC tracking via GUObjectArray. UPROPERTY for GC references.

o: UHT generates: StaticClass() function, class flags (EClassCastFlags), CDO (Class Default Object), reflection data
questions.a: UHT generates: StaticClass() function, class flags (EClassCastFlags), CDO (Class Default Object), reflection data
questions.e: UCLASS = UE's reflection. StaticClass() returns UClass*. Cast<T> uses class flags. CDO = template instance with default property values.

questions.q: What is the Class Default Object (CDO)?
o: The default instance of a UClass — created at class registration. Used as template: new objects copy CDO's property values.
questions.a: The default instance of a UClass — created at class registration. Used as template: new objects copy CDO's property values.
questions.e: CDO = prototype object. Created once per class at engine init. NewObject<T>() copies CDO's properties. Editor defaults = CDO properties.

questions.q: What is the UObject creation path?
o: NewObject<T>(): allocates memory, copies CDO properties, calls constructor, registers with GC, calls PostInitProperties
questions.a: NewObject<T>(): allocates memory, copies CDO properties, calls constructor, registers with GC, calls PostInitProperties
questions.e: NewObject = UE's object factory. Never use 'new' for UObjects. NewObject handles GC registration, CDO copy, property initialization.

questions.q: How does UE garbage collection know an object is reachable?
o: Mark-and-sweep: starts from root set (UGameInstance, UWorld), follows UPROPERTY references. Unmarked = unreachable = delete.
questions.a: Mark-and-sweep: starts from root set (UGameInstance, UWorld), follows UPROPERTY references. Unmarked = unreachable = delete.
questions.e: UE GC: mark phase (traverse UPROPERTY graph from roots) → sweep phase (delete unmarked). Raw pointers DON'T prevent GC — must be UPROPERTY.
